整个地球的历史，气候变化将动物和植物物种推向灭绝。大约2.5亿年前，全球变暖在巨大的火山喷发引发的突出，在二叠纪期间占据了所有海洋物种的96％，作为科学杂志细节。现在，一些研究人员认为气候变化与古代人类相同。
 由Pasquale Raia的一名研究团队在意大利的那不勒斯联邦馆联盟II联盟，在过去的500万年中，在意大利交叉引用了近3000名人类物种考古记录，降雨量和其他天气数据。他们在一个地球上发表的调查结果表明，全球冷却事件影响了人类的演化，驾驶三个现代人类表兄弟灭绝。
 虽然一些科学家认为古代人类的化石记录并不足够详述，但是，Raia认为他的团队的研究为人们提供了人类演变的过去的警告。
 “令人担心的是，与地球上的任何其他物种相比，我们的祖先不那么令人印象不那么令人印象深刻，无法抵制气候变化，”他在尤瑞卡尔特发布的一份新闻发表中说。
  现代人类称为Homo Sapiens，是Homo属的一部分，其存在至少280万年。在此期间，几种不同种类的同性恋者生活在地球上，并且考古记录表明，有些人有聪明的控制火灾，建立社交网络，制作石材工具并创造衣服。虽然这些迹象显示了技术和认知技能的线索，但只有H. Sapiens幸存下来。
 为什么？到目前为止，没有人知道。研究人员在一个地球上说明“尽管此事巨大的重要性，但仍然没有一致的解释。”
 Raia是一个进化的生物学家，与十几个人进行调查。他们删除了一个化石数据库，其中包含2,754个考古记录，以绘制六种不同的同性恋物种随着时间的推移，如萨皮斯报告。研究人员还使用统计建模技术称为过去的气候仿真器，在六种物种的位置重建古老的气候条件，返回500万年。 
接下来，他们分析了特定时间点的环境条件，发现该气候突然发生三种物种 -  H.Ereectus，H.Heidelbergensis和H. Neanderthalensis  - 根据科学家的最后一个已知的出现在化石记录中。具体而言，对于所有三个来说，它变得越来越冷，H. Eerectus和H.Heeandergensis和H. Neanderthalensis的H. Eerectus和干燥器。
 研究人员使用了保护生物学领域的技术来评估这些物种是否可能易受气候变化的影响。例如，H. Erectus在现在居住在现在的印度尼西亚的Java岛，生活在温暖，潮湿的条件下。但根据气候模型，冰川期可能会为物种适应而产生温度过于寒冷。
 他们的分析表明，在失踪之前，气候变化声称超过一半以上的H. Erectus和H.Heidelbergensis利基 - 以及H. Neanderthalensis Niche的四分之一。改变栖息地和越来越感冒的可能有限的食物来源，并威胁到更习惯于加热渠道的人的生存。
  辩论愤怒如何以及为什么古代人类消失了。这项研究没有参与的几位研究人员指出，人类演化的化石记录稀疏，更少于及时可靠。其他人说，在给定的位置，僵化的遗骸仍然可能并不意味着物种当时灭绝 - 这可能只是意味着科学家报告尚未发现从后来日期的化石。来自这些同一地点和时间点的动物和植物的进一步分析和研究可能会借鉴 - 或不 - 对raia的假设。
 在尤瑞卡尔特新闻发表声明中，Raia表示，他同意他的同事的评论，但他的团队的主要结果“在所有假设下保持了真实”，并作为一个警告，因为这个星球面临前所未有的气候变化。 “我们对气候变化效果的规律感到惊讶，”Raia说。 “它是晶体清晰的，对于灭绝的物种，仅限于它们，那种气候条件只是在灭绝之前太极端，只是在那个特定的时刻。”
 这是否意味着现代人类可能会灭绝？据史密森尼国家自然历史博物馆介绍，人类演变发生在地球历史中最戏判中最戏剧性的不稳定之一。古代人类面临着许多极端挑战，包括疾病，伤害和捕食，以及适应幸存的物种。
 但对于现代人类来说，时间可能不多了。联合国表示，该地球少于10年来防止不可逆转的损害。即使人类可以借鉴技术知识，他们依赖于食物的植物和动物可能会屈服于更高的温度，更酸性的海洋和其他环境的转变，创造了一个多米诺骨牌效果，可以更多地使地球生命变得更加越来越多作为科学报告中详述的研究人员，岌岌可危。 
“我个人将此作为雷鸣的警告消息。 过去的气候变化使HOMO脆弱和渗透在过去，这可能只是再次发生，“Raia说。
note_en:
Throughout Earth’s history, climate change has pushed animal and plant species into extinction. About 250 million years ago, global warming triggered by massive volcanic eruptions wiped out 96 percent of all marine species during the Permian period, as  Science magazine details. Now, some researchers think climate change did the same to ancient humans.
 A research team led by Pasquale Raia of the University of Naples Federico II in Italy cross-referenced nearly 3,000 archaeological records of human species with temperature, rainfall and other weather data over the past 5 million years. Their findings, published in  One Earth, suggest that global cooling episodes influenced  human evolution, driving three of modern human’s cousins to extinction.
 Although some scientists think the fossil record for ancient humans isn’t detailed enough to draw such conclusions, Raia thinks his team’s research offers people today a warning from human evolution’s past.
 “It is worrisome to discover that our ancestors, which were no less impressive in terms of mental power as compared to any other species on Earth, could not resist climate change,” he said in a press statement published by  EurekAlert.
  Modern humans, called Homo sapiens, are part of the genus Homo, which has existed for at least 2.8 million years. Several different species of Homo lived on Earth during that time, and the archaeological record shows that some had the smarts to control fire, establish social networks, make stone tools and create clothing. Although these signs reveal clues to technological and cognitive skills, only H. sapiens survived.
 Why? So far, no one knows. The researchers note in One Earth that “no consistent explanation has yet been advanced, despite the enormous importance of the matter.”
 Raia, an evolutionary biologist, teamed up with more than a dozen other scientists to investigate. They tapped a fossil database that contained 2,754 archaeological records to map where and when six different Homo species lived over time, as  Sapiens reports. The researchers also used a statistical modeling technique called a past climate emulator to reconstruct ancient climate conditions at the locations where the six species lived, going back 5 million years.
 Next, they analyzed the environmental conditions at particular timepoints and found that the climate changed suddenly for three species — H. erectus, H. heidelbergensis and H. neanderthalensis — just before their last known appearance in the fossil record, according to  The Scientist. Specifically, it became colder for all three, wetter for H. erectus and drier for both H. heildelbergensis and H. neanderthalensis.
 The researchers used techniques from the field of conservation biology to assess whether these species may have been vulnerable to climate change. For instance, H. erectus, which lived approximately 110,000 years ago on what is now the Indonesian island of Java, lived in warm, humid conditions. But according to the climate model, a glacial period may have created temperatures too cold for the species to adapt.
 Their analysis shows that climate change claimed more than half of the H. erectus and H. heidelbergensis niche prior to their disappearance — along with a quarter of the H. neanderthalensis niche. Changing habitats and increasing cold likely limited food sources and threatened survival for those more accustomed to warmer climes.
  Debates rage over how and why ancient humans disappeared. Several researchers not involved in this study have pointed out that the fossil record for human evolution is sparse and less reliable the further back in time it goes. Others say the last appearance of fossilized remains at a given location may not mean the species went extinct at that time — it may simply mean that fossils from later dates have not yet been discovered, as The Scientist reports. Further analysis and studies of animals and plants from these same locations and timepoints may lend credence — or not — to Raia’s hypothesis.
 In the EurekAlert press statement, Raia says he agrees with the comments from his colleagues but that his team’s main findings “hold true under all assumptions” and serve as a warning as the planet faces unprecedented climate changes. “We were surprised by the regularity of the effect of climate change,” Raia says. “It was crystal clear, for the extinct species and for them only, that climatic conditions were just too extreme just before extinction and only in that particular moment.”
 Does this mean that modern humans could become extinct? According to the  Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History, human evolution occurred during one of the most dramatic eras of climate instability in Earth’s history. Ancient humans faced many extreme challenges, including disease, injury and predation, and those species that adapted survived.
 But for modern humans, time may be running out. The  United Nations says the planet has fewer than 10 years to prevent irreversible damage. And even if humans can draw upon technological know-how to stay alive, the plants and animals they depend on for food could succumb to higher temperatures, more acidic oceans and other environmentals shifts, creating a domino effect that could make life on Earth increasingly more precarious, as researchers detailed in  Scientific Reports.
 “I personally take this as a thunderous warning message. Climate change made Homo vulnerable and hapless in the past, and this may just be happening again,” Raia says.
title: 气候变化可能会将古代人类推向灭绝
title_en: Climate change may have pushed ancient humans into extinction

最后，我们认为计算机愿景成为推进艺术体育分析研究的界限的最有前途的途径之一。通过纯粹从视频中检测事件，在计算机视觉社区中研究的主题（例如，参见以下调查和纸纸用于其他参考文献），潜在的应用范围是巨大的。通过将事件与特定帧相关联，视频可搜索，并且更有用（例如，自动突出生成可能）。反过来，足球视频为计算机愿景提供了一个有趣的应用域。大量的足球视频满足了现代AI技术的先决条件。虽然每个足球视频都不同，但是设置不会变化很大，这使得任务是锐化AI算法的理想选择。第三方提供商还存在提供可以在训练视频模型中有用的手工标记的事件数据，并且耗时为生成，因此监督和无监督的算法都可以用于足球事件检测。例如，图1（b）提供了由监督方法培训的深度学习模型的程式化可视化，以纯粹从视频中识别目标事件（例如，踢）。
 高级AI技术在足球中的应用有可能彻底改变跨越许多轴的游戏，适用于球员，决策者，粉丝和广播公司。这种进展也将是重要的，因为它们也承担进一步民主体系本身的潜力（例如，而不是依靠人侦察员/专家的判断，而且可以使用计算机愿景等技术来量化来自的球员的技能代表的地区，来自较低级别的联赛等）。我们认为，足球微观越来越高级的AI技术的发展可能适用于更广泛的域。为此，我们共同组织（有几个外部组织者）今年晚些时候对AI的AI 2021讲师2021讲师，我们欢迎有兴趣的研究人员参加。对于对本主题感兴趣的研究人员来说，公开可用的数据集由STATSBOMB（DataSet Link）和更广泛的研究社区（DataSet Link）等分析公司提供。此外，本文提供了全面的研究概述了该领域的研究。

note_en:
Creating testing environments to help progress AI research out of the lab and into the real world is immensely challenging. Given AI’s long association with games, it is perhaps no surprise that sports presents an exciting opportunity, offering researchers a testbed in which an AI-enabled system can assist humans in making complex, real-time decisions in a multiagent environment with dozens of dynamic, interacting individuals.
 The rapid growth of sports data collection means we are in the midst of a remarkably important era for sports analytics. The availability of sports data is increasing in both quantity and granularity, transitioning from the days of aggregate high-level statistics and sabermetrics to more refined data such as event stream information (e.g., annotated passes or shots), high-fidelity player positional information, and  on-body sensors. However, the field of sports analytics has only recently started to harness machine learning and AI for both understanding and advising human decision-makers in sports. In our  recent paper published in collaboration with Liverpool Football Club (LFC) in JAIR, we envision the future landscape of sports analytics using a combination of statistical learning, video understanding, and game theory. We illustrate football, in particular, is a useful microcosm for studying AI research, offering benefits in the longer-term to decision-makers in sports in the form of an automated video-assistant coach (AVAC) system (Figure 1(A)).
      In comparison to some other sports, football has been rather late with starting to systematically collect large sets of data for scientific analytics purposes aiming to progress teams’ gameplay. This is for several reasons, with the most prominent being that there are far less controllable settings of the game compared to other sports (large outdoor pitch, dynamic game, etc.), and also the dominant credo to rely mainly on human specialists with track records and experience in professional football. On these lines, Arrigo Sacchi, a successful Italian football coach and manager who never played professional football in his career, responded to criticism over his lack of experience with his  famous quote when becoming a coach at Milan in 1987: “I never realised that to be a jockey you had to be a horse first.”
 Football Analytics poses challenges that are well suited for a wide variety of AI techniques, coming from the intersection of 3 fields: computer vision, statistical learning and game theory (visualised in Figure 2). While these fields are individually useful for football analytics, their benefits become especially tangible when combined: players need to take sequential decision-making in the presence of other players (cooperative and adversarial) and as such game theory, a theory of interactive decision making, becomes highly relevant. Moreover, tactical solutions to particular in-game situations can be learnt based on in-game and specific player representations, which makes statistical learning a highly relevant area. Finally, players can be tracked and game scenarios can be recognised automatically from widely-available image and video inputs.
      The AVAC system we envision is situated within the microcosm that is formed by the intersection of these three research fields (Figure 2). In our research in this exciting domain, we not only lay out a roadmap for scientific and engineering problems that can be tackled for years to come, but we also present new original results at the crossroads of game theoretic analysis, statistical learning, and computer vision to illustrate what this exciting area has to offer to football.
  Game theory plays an important role in the study of sports, enabling theoretical grounding of players’ behavioral strategies. In the case of football, many of its scenarios can actually be modeled as zero-sum games, which have been studied extensively since the inception of game theory. For example, here we model the penalty kick situation as a two-player asymmetric game, where the kicker’s strategies may be neatly categorised as left, center, or right shots. To study this problem, we augment game-theoretic analysis in the penalty kick scenario with  Player Vectors, which summarise the playing styles of individual football players. With such representations of individual players, we are able to group kickers with similar playing styles, and then conduct game-theoretic analysis on the group-level (Figure 3). Our results show that the identified shooting strategies of different groups are statistically distinct. For example, we find that one group prefers to shoot to the left corner of the goal mouth, while another tends to shoot to the left and right corners more evenly. Such insights may help goalkeepers diversify their defense strategies when playing against different types of players. Building on this game-theoretic view, one can consider the durative nature of football by analysing it in the form of temporally-extended games, use this to advise tactics to individual players, or even go further to optimise the overall team strategy.
      On the side of statistical learning, representation learning has yet to be fully exploited in sports analytics, which would enable informative summarisation of the behavior of individual players and football teams. Moreover, we believe that the interaction between game theory and statistical learning would catalyse advances in sports analytics further. In the above penalty kick scenario, for instance, augmenting the analysis with player-specific statistics (Player Vectors) provided deeper insights into how various types of players behave or make decisions about their actions in the penalty kick scenario. As another example of this, one can study ' ghosting', which refers to a particular data driven analysis of how players should have acted in hindsight in sports analytics (which bears connections to the notion of regret in online learning and game theory). The ghosting model suggests alternative player trajectories for a given play, e.g., based on the league average or a selected team. Predicted trajectories are usually visualised as a translucent layer over the original play, hence the term 'ghosting' (see Figure 4 for a visual example). Generative trajectory prediction models allow us to gain insights by analysing key situations of a game and how they might have played out differently. These models also bear potential in predicting the implications of a tactical change, a key player's injury, or substitution on the own team's performance along with the opposition's response to such a change.
      Finally, we consider computer vision to be one of the most promising avenues for advancing the boundaries of state of the art sports analytics research. By detecting events purely from video, a topic that has been well-studied in the computer vision community (e.g., see the following  survey and our paper for additional references), the potential range of application is enormous. By associating events with particular frames, videos become searchable and ever more useful (e.g., automatic highlight generation becomes possible). Football video, in turn, offers an interesting application domain for computer vision. The large numbers of football videos satisfies a prerequisite for modern AI techniques. While each football video is different, the settings do not vary greatly, which makes the task ideal for sharpening AI algorithms. Third-party providers also exist to furnish hand-labelled event data that can be useful in training video models and are time consuming to generate, so both supervised and unsupervised algorithms can be used for football event detection. Figure 1(B), for example, provides a stylised visualisation of a deep learning model trained with supervised methods to recognise target events (e.g., kicks) purely from video.
 The application of advanced AI techniques to football has the potential to revolutionise the game across many axes, for players, decision-makers, fans, and broadcasters. Such advances will also be important as they also bear potential to further democratise the sport itself (e.g., rather than relying on judgement calls from in-person scouts/experts, one may use techniques such as computer vision to quantify skillsets of players from under-represented regions, those from lower-level leagues, etc.). We believe that the development of increasingly advanced AI techniques afforded by the football microcosm might be applicable to broader domains. To this end, we are co-organising (with several external organisers) an  IJCAI 2021 workshop on AI for Sports Analytics later this year, which we welcome interested researchers to attend. For researchers interested in this topic, publicly available datasets have been made available both by analytics companies such as StatsBomb ( dataset link) and the wider research community ( dataset link). Furthermore, the paper provides a comprehensive overview of research in this domain.
   Work done as a collaboration with contributors: Karl Tuyls, Shayegan Omidshafiei, Paul Muller, Zhe Wang, Jerome Connor, Daniel Hennes, Ian Graham, William Spearman, Tim Waskett, Dafydd Steele, Pauline Luc, Adria Recasens, Alexandre Galashov, Gregory Thornton, Romuald Elie, Pablo Sprechmann, Pol Moreno, Kris Cao, Marta Garnelo, Praneet Dutta, Michal Valko, Nicolas Heess, Alex Bridgland, Julien Perolat, Bart De Vylder, Ali Eslami, Mark Rowland, Andrew Jaegle, Yi Yang, Remi Munos, Trevor Back, Razia Ahamed, Simon Bouton, Nathalie Beauguerlange, Jackson Broshear, Thore Graepel, and Demis Hassabis.
title: 通过AI研究推进体育分析
title_en: Advancing Sports Analytics Through AI Research

赢得诺贝尔奖一次，没有简单的壮举让别人赢得两次！第一个这样做的人是化学家和物理学家玛丽居里，因为你们很多人都可能已经知道。
  同样，John Bardeen于1956年首次赢得了物理学的着名奖，然后在1972年赢得了着名的物理奖。虽然，如果你碰巧观看大爆炸理论，它可能会令人惊讶的是，他是专业的工程师。
   BARDEEN（1908-1991）分别于1928年和1929年完成了他的学士学位和硕士学位。然后，他被海湾石油公司雇用了四年。
  然而，他对物理学的热爱是完整的，并敦促他回到学校。因此，他于1933年注册了普林斯顿大学学习物理和数学。
  在那里，他在诺贝尔劳特尤金瓦涅瓦的指导下撰写了一篇关于固态物理学的论文。 1935年毕业后，他被选为哈佛大学的初中，他举行了三年。
   1939年，第二次世界大战爆发了，约翰再也无法促进了他个人的研究兴趣。 1945年10月在贝尔实验室开始工作后，战争突破了。
  除了同事威廉·施谢和沃尔特布拉特以及1947年的第一晶体管。然而，当震惊试图采取大部分对本发明的信誉时，他们的关系感到厌恶。 
Shockley防止了Bardeen和Brattain在晶体管技术上进一步工作。因此，约翰留下了1951年的贝尔实验室，并接受了伊利诺伊大学的提议，研究超导。
  1956年，他与震撼利用的物理学分享了诺贝尔奖，并在晶体管上工作。今天，正如您所知，大多数计算技术都在没有晶体管的情况下难以想象。
  当巴加丁只带三个孩子到奖品分销仪式之一时，瑞典国王嘲笑他，牛肉坦率地回答道：＆＃34;下次我会把它们带到瑞典。＆＃34;
   1957年，约翰写了一系列超导理论，以及莱昂库珀和约翰·施莱弗尔。它分别迎来了新的交通和医疗技术（如Maglev和MRI）的新时代。
  15年后，约翰在1972年将他的三个孩子送到诺贝尔奖励仪式时，他将他赐给瑞典国王的承诺。
  约翰于1975年担任伊利诺伊大学的教授。1983年，索尼公司在约翰的发明内欠了大部分商业成功，在大学创造了一个名誉约翰·巴德·巴德·巴德教授的荣誉。
  它类似于剑桥大学的卢西尼亚数学教授的＆＃39;一座董事会成立于1663年，由牛顿，迪拉克和霍金等图标举行。 
在1988年的采访中，当Bardeen被要求评论宗教时，他说：＆＃34;我不是一个宗教人士，所以不要非常想到它。＆＃34; 约翰是一个非常卑微的科学家，捐赠了他的诺贝尔奖金。 他喜欢为没有意识到他的科学成就的邻居举办追捕园区。 如果您列出了人们的名单 - 政客，科学家，运动员等 - 对20世纪的影响最大，约翰＆＃39的名字肯定会使它成为前十名。 因为没有他的工作，没有一个现代技术是可能的。
note_en:
Winning the Nobel Prize once is no easy feat let alone winning it twice! The first ever person to do so was chemist and physicist  Marie Curie as many of you might already know.
  Similarly, John Bardeen has won the prestigious prize for physics first in 1956 and then in 1972. Although, if you happen to watch  The Big Bang Theory, it might come off as surprising that he was an engineer by profession.
   Bardeen (1908-1991) completed his bachelor and master degrees in electrical engineering in 1928 and 1929 respectively. He was then employed by Gulf Oil Corporation where he worked for four years.
  However, his love for physics was intact and urged him to go back to school. Therefore, he enrolled at Princeton University to study physics and mathematics in 1933.
  There he wrote a thesis on solid-state physics under the guidance of Nobel laureate Eugene Wigner. After graduating in 1935, he was chosen as Junior Fellow at Harvard University, a position he held for three years.
   In 1939, the second world war broke out and John could no longer facilitate his individual research interests. The big break came after the war in October 1945 when he started working at Bell Labs.
  Along with colleagues William Shockley and Walter Brattain, John invented the first transistor in 1947. Their relationship, however, soured when Shockley tried to take most of the credit for the invention.
    Shockley prevented both Bardeen and Brattain from working any further on the transistor technologies. So, John left Bell Labs in 1951 and accepted an offer from the University of Illinois to study superconductivity.
  In 1956, he shared the Nobel Prize in physics with Shockley and Brattain for their work on the transistor. Today, as you might know, most of computing technologies are unimaginable without the transistor.
  When Bardeen brought only one of his three children to the prize distribution ceremony, the King of Sweden ridiculed him, to which Bardeen candidly replied: "Next time I will bring them all to Sweden."
   In 1957, John wrote a theory of superconductivity along with Leon Cooper and John Schrieffer. It ushered a new era of transportation and medical technologies such as MagLev and MRI respectively.
  15 years later, John kept the promise he made to the King of Sweden when he took his three children to the Nobel Prize distribution ceremony in 1972.
  John stayed as a professor at University of Illinois until 1975. In 1983, Sony corporation, which owed much of its commercial success to inventions by John, created an honorary John Bardeen professorship at the university.
  It's similar to the Lucasian professor of mathematics at Cambridge University, a chair founded in 1663 and held by icons like  Newton,  Dirac and  Hawking.
   In a 1988 interview, when Bardeen was asked to comment on religion, he said: "I am  not a religious person and so do not think about it very much." John was a very humble scientist who donated much of his Nobel Prize money. He enjoyed hosting cookouts for neighbours who were unaware of his scientific achievements.  If you make a list of people – politicians, scientists, sportspersons, etc – who have had the greatest impact on the 20th century, John's name would certainly make it to the top ten. Because, without his work, none of the modern technologies would be possible.

note:
纽约时报最近造成了轰动的时候，在一篇关于大流行书销售的文章中，它透露了“98％的书籍，2020年发布的出版商出售少于5,000份。”
 虽然这个统计数据很令人震惊，但它不是新的信息。人们不读书 - 那些不买它们的书。更糟糕的是，“公布发布的书籍”只是“成功案例” - 索斯书籍，赢得了难以获得的大四四个出版合同 - 那些已经是一小部分出版市场。
 根据书店的说法，它看起来整体上的书籍出版市场，2020年有260万本在线销售，其中268只销售了100,000多份 - 只有0.0001％的书籍。到目前为止，卖出0到1,000份的可能性更可能 - 去年有260万人（96％）。
  （即使在高端，也只有11本书销售超过500,000份 - 当您认为这部10个最佳Netflix薄膜看到超过6800万次景观时，这是掌握的。事实证明，书籍是媒介没有观众 - 或者相反，他们有一个非常适用的观众。）
 作为作者，这是令人痛苦的。如果我可以花两到三年的写作小说，我最好的情况是它在亚马逊上销售了几百份拷贝，也许是时候面对音乐，并意识到写作书籍的编织或播放竖琴 - 没什么比爱好。我可以在周末娱乐的东西，但绝不应该希望获得生活。
 然而，我忍不住怀疑中间的某个地方是一个甜蜜的现货：卖出1000到100,000份的书 - 这是由少数少数人所喜爱的。这可能不会卖得足以使其在出版合同和剧本选项的大世界中，但可以卖得足够的盈利 - 如果只使用创造经济技术。
   毫无疑问，目前的出版模式不适用于作者。 
如果大多数书籍在0到10,000份之间出售，那么大多数书籍在每年的价格为0到42,000美元之间。一旦提交人销售了传统上的45,000份或24,000份自我发布的副本，那么每年薪水才会发生一旦发布了45,000份 - 并且有很多书籍销售许多人（见上文图表）。更不用说，一个提交人必须一年来一年来保持薪水。
  许多作者希望与四大出版社确保合同将提供更多的营销和销售这本书的更多副本，但现实是传统出版商正在寻找一个肯定的事情。他们想要一个已经拥有现有平台的作者，可以保证受众。如果作者有那个，他们可能会更好地独自走出来。
 作为Rachel Deahl，每周发布者的新闻总监曾告诉我：“出版商一直在寻找具有真正大平台的人。如果Kanye West将发布一本书，他已经有了一位大观众 - 你不必为他建立观众。拥有内置观众的人，可以联系到他们并说我＆＃39;米发表一本书，那本书可以立即成为一个畅销书。
 “那个＆＃39;真的很难在小说中。在小说中，出版商正在看长期。他们想建立一个作者，所以他们可以成为斯蒂芬国王或吉莉安·弗林。因为现在那些作者有巨大的平台。“不幸的是，“大多数书籍Don＆＃39; T即使有很多支持也成功了。结合没有营销或宣传，您的书是您的书籍＆＃39; T畅销。“
   如果所有这一切都听起来非常令人沮丧，也许在我的第三点有希望。毕竟，三个书籍仍然在1,000-100,000范围内出售 - 如果使用创造经济技术，那么这足够受到货币化的受众。
 作为进入智慧的国家：它只需要1000名真正的粉丝花费100美元/年的创造者获得100,000美元/年的工资 - 每年有83,397本书，至少有1,000名真正的粉丝。理论上，作者可以每周发布新章，每月收取8美元或9美元，每年赚10万美元 - 从只有1000名读者赚取10万美元。
 非小说着作者已经在这样做了。正如Alexey Guzey的这个图表所证明的那样，有大量的家用作家，他们为他们的追随者提供了优质的非小说内容，并每年用数十万美元货币化，在某些情况下，读者订阅！ 
但是小说也是一样的？这是一个尚未答造的问题。家庭中有一些串行小说作家 - 但没有支付。帕勒顿有成千上万的有偿小说作者，但只有25美元赚了超过1000美元，只有六个以上$ 2,000 /月，只有一个人收入超过5,000美元/月（她已经是一个畅销书作者）。
 这里的真正成功的故事是N.K. jemisin在收到传统的出版合同之前，在帕勒顿赚取5,068美元/月的出版小说，而不是那条路线。但她是我们唯一真正的案例研究。
 如果我们还没有足够的案例研究来确定虚构是否可以成功序列化并货币化，如非小说内容，我们至少知道有一个市场。 Wattpad目前拥有9000万用户，平均每场比赛平均52分钟阅读书籍在线 - 大多数千禧年和Gen Z.同样，我们自己的档案（AO3）在线粉彩故事后拥有4000万用户。
 在这两个网站上，新章节出来，因为作家写下他们和追随者在实时阅读和评论这些章节。两个平台的问题是读者免费读取，作者没有从他们的工作中获得任何东西 - 但是开始改变。随着2019年两年的Wattpad的付费故事推出，读者开始使用微挖的章节支付 - 它需要三个“硬币”来解锁书籍的下一部分。
 不幸的是，作者没有决定他们的内容是否是付费故事的一部分，程序-Wattpad是根据本书在自由迭代中执行的方式进行的。两年来，瓦特PAD宣布仅在550名作家中达到1万美元的作者盈利。
  所有的东西都是平等的，每位作者的总收入只有1,818美元，两年期间 - 所有事情都不平等。更有可能的情况是，这550分中的一小部分弥补了大部分的盈利，与我们其他人的底部有其余的群集。
 一个Wattpad作者希望保持匿名的作家告诉我她的书在去年邀请去支付之前在平台上达到了200万毫无挪用阅读。从那时起，她已经获得了几百万元的阅读，并且在收益中取得了500美元/月，她的最高月份以1,000美元。而这与YA浪漫小说 - 网站上最好的执行类别之一。 
如果有2000万人愿意免费阅读一本书，并且愿意为此付出几百百万，但作者仍然只有12,000美元或13,000美元的一年 - 小说家作为一个职业的希望是什么？即使使用创造科学技术，也没有足够的人愿意为小说作者为小说付出代理人来实现这样做。
 亚马逊比任何人都更了解这一点，也就是说，他们在2021年4月宣布的原因是Kindle Vella-A Wattpad竞争对手的推出，允许作者顺序发布他们的书籍并获得50％的读者支付的读者来解锁下一章。因为有这么多万人我们将在亚马逊上倒在亚马逊上的希望能成为下一个丹布朗，亚马逊将获得一个50％的利润的漂亮便士 - 即使我们只出售200份拷贝。
 他们就像健身房会员资格，以为每个人都没有出现。
 唉，我是那些能够＆＃39; t帮助但坚持在梦中的人之一 - 我留下了一个微小的一个想法的热点：我们曾经认为没有人会支付5美元/月来订阅个人作家，他们可以支付4美元/月，订阅所有纽约时报，因为证明是错误的。
 也许这就是为什么，用我的最后一个蜡烛，我仍然希望我们尚未证明对小说的案例。如果你＆＃39; ll沉迷于我的幻想一段时间......
   如果我仍然留下了一些希望创造者经济可能为小说作者工作，那么这个问题是：这是小说小说的最佳平台？
 当然，Wattpad和AO3对于那些想要读过收入的人来说是明显的竞争者。两个平台都有一个巨大的粉丝赌场，并提供作者一个平台，他们可以为他们的工作开发以下一个平台。作为一个额外的好处，在任何一种情况下，作者都保留了他们的工作权利。 
（这与亚马逊Vella不正确。如果作者选择使用该平台发布他们的小说，它就无法在其他任何地方发布 - 而且，亚马逊在这里赚取50％的收益，就像特许权使用费一样陡峭。特别没有任何额外营销和未知市场的承诺。）
 由于这一点，谈到序列含量的序列含量时，别人和帕勒顿有边缘（尽管WattPad可能是竞争者，但根据他们是否打开付费计划）。由于两个平台都允许作者保持其工作的权利，因此无法阻止这些作者将他们的书籍放在维特果，AO3和点燃之后。
 坚定地确定我将自己的小说序列化为实验，从2021年8月到2022年8月释放一章，我创建了一个家喻户手和帕勒顿账户来测试水域。最终，虽然两者都有份情，但也不适合小说。
 Patreon没有免费定价层，这意味着我必须在尝试卖出之前在其他地方（家喻户口，社交媒体等）建立我的平台。 suppl允许我有一个免费的新闻通讯，可以将upsells进入付费版本，但我无法访问我可以使用Patreon的所有定价层。 （Suppl只允许我有一个每月订阅费，加上一个“团块”捐赠桶。）
 句号具有正确的想法 - 将受众放在一个平台上并使它们更容易，而不是将某人从一个平台移动到另一个平台。但我知道定价层是必不可少的。毕竟，为什么读者会支付5美元/月，每月读一下一本书的一章，当时他们可以在$ 1.99上购买一本全书？ （这就是为什么有大量作家在Patreon上写小说，赚取200美元/月 - 以及大量的Kindle作者，获得200美元）。
 必须增加值。这就是为什么我希望我的读者可以选择加入独家在线社区;在我的书的致谢部分中提到;写下我的书的前言;收到签名，收集者版，这是一本书的完整副本;最后参加我的包装派对;或者以哥特式庄园在法国，以后将幽灵故事写在一起。
 毕竟，这是如何n.k. jemisin达到了她的帕勒顿成功。她有五个超级班士支付100美元/月，她的书籍和九份的印刷副本和90美元/月的签名 - 作者副本，当他们是订阅者。从她的前14名粉丝们只有95​​0美元/月的收入！作者不能错过那种赞助。 （想想Pottermore-粉丝只是继续给予。） 
此时，我决定使用Suppl序列化我的小说 - 整个过程已经建立在内部，并已被证明为非小说作者工作 - 但我仍然不知道我是否会通过我自己的自定义添加定价层条纹账户，Venmo Business帐户，甚至通过与Patreon Pricing层组合的家喻户晓账户。我想我仍然希望家具在8月1日发布的时候，家庭会在自己的房子上出来。
 我有信心，家人会到达那里。他们有一个概念证明，很多投资进来，就在本周宣布将部分添加到出版物 - 这将让我与我的小说章节分开。
 即使在这些新兴技术的帮助下，创造者经济也可能不适用于小说作者 - 除了电视的发明之外，没有其他人真正拥有。但是，无论如何，我会跑一个实验，看看会发生什么。因为你永远不知道，也许毕竟希望虚构的希望 - 我们还没有想到它。
  一如既往，谢谢你这么多阅读，我会在两周内见到你！
     我一直在收集和追随当前在家食上写小说的作者，希望在我准备发射我的时候从他们那里学习。以下是我发现的一些：
 Mark Starlin的星期一早晨Mark是我最喜欢的新闻通讯之一。他写了短暂的，幽默，小说故事，让我想起杰克·勤奋的深思熟虑（记住那些？）。我几乎每次都大声笑。
 猎犬的咬合股票短暂的哥特式故事与Zoe Harrington的哥特式小说章节交流。 
太阳能流动是K.B的Sauminic Thriller。 贝利，我迫不及待地想读书。  番红花和熊是关于katie康拉德的巫婆和她的猫的小说。  Inykaethel是吸血鬼的回忆录，即一旦它开始阅读，我非常兴奋。  如果你想知道我的命令，答案是审美的。 我想让它做那个副书，以便看起来像一群鹅。  我们所有人上面（以及更多的家喻户手）都有一个discord服务器，我们共享我们所有的家喻写写作秘密。 你可以在这里加入。  我决定不再告诉你我正在读的是什么，因为我了解到我读过一本书的第一部分，然后决定我不想再读了它，我做了很多次。 所以我只分享我完成的书。  自从两周前曾经上次说过，这是：没有书籍。 很大程度上是因为我现在正在阅读大约五本书，它已经失控了。 我下次会有更多报告。 
哦，但我非常喜欢我在床上发现的古董书籍; 完全是关于鲜花的早餐。 我最喜欢的外带之一是牡丹原产于中国，据说恶作剧若虫藏在他们的花瓣中。 这是一个非常愉快的书。  我仍在研究我目前正在努力的小说中的第三章。 通常不会带我这段时间来完成一章，但我想知道我想要做出难以理解的事情，这是我在写下剩下的剩余之前所需的事情。 关于未来的写作可以去任何地方，我都被所有的可能性所淹没！ 对他们来说非常兴奋。  我还写了这份通讯，为我的时事通告做了一些更多的广告，希望促进我的第一本书将于8月份首次亮相（如此兴奋/紧张）。 我很快就会分享我的结果。
note_en:
The New York Times caused a stir recently when, in  an article about pandemic book sales, it disclosed that “98 percent of the books that publishers released in 2020 sold fewer than 5,000 copies.”
 Though this statistic was shocking to many, it is not new information.  People don’t read books—and the ones that do aren’t buying them. To make matters worse, “books that publishers released” are only the “success stories”—those books that scored hard-won Big-Four publishing contracts—and those are already a small piece of the book publishing market.
 According to  Bookstat, which looks at the book publishing market as a whole, there were 2.6 million books sold online in 2020 and only 268 of them sold more than 100,000 copies—that’s only 0.0001 percent of books.  By far, the more likely thing is to sell between 0 and 1,000 copies—and there were 2.6 million of those last year (96 percent).
  (Even on the high end, there were only 11 books that sold more than 500,000 copies—which is paltry when you consider that the 10 best-performing Netflix films  saw more than 68 million views. As it turns out, books as a medium just don’t have an audience—or rather, they have a very niche audience.)
 As an author, this is  distressing. If I can spend two to three years writing a novel and my best case scenario is having it sell a couple hundred copies on Amazon, perhaps it’s time to face the music and realize that writing books—like knitting or playing the harp—is nothing more than a hobby. Something I can do for fun on the weekends but should never hope to earn a living from.
 And yet, I can’t help but wonder if somewhere in the middle lies a sweet spot: books that sell between 1,000 and 100,000 copies—that are loved by a small by devoted few. That might not sell enough to make it in the big world of publishing contracts and screenplay options, but  could sell enough to be profitable—if only creator economy technologies are used.
   There’s no doubt that the current publishing model doesn’t work for authors.
 If most books sell between 0 and 10,000 copies, then most books are earning between $0 and $42,000 in a year. An annual salary of $100,000 only happens once an author has sold 45,000 copies traditionally or 24,000 copies self-published—and there are very few books that sell that many (see above chart). Not to mention, an author would have to come out with one book a year to maintain that salary.
  Many authors hope that securing a contract with a Big Four publishing house will provide more marketing and sell more copies of the book, but the reality is that traditional publishers are looking for a sure thing. They want an author who already has an existing platform and can guarantee an audience. And if the author has that, they might be better off going it alone.
 As Rachel Deahl, news director for  Publishers Weekly once told me: “Publishers are always looking for people with really big platforms. If Kanye West is going to publish a book, he's got a big audience already—you don't have to build an audience for him. Someone with a built-in audience, who can reach out to them and say I'm publishing a book, that book can become a bestselling book immediately.
 “That's really hard in fiction. In fiction, publishers are looking at the long-term. They want to build an author so they can become a Stephen King or Gillian Flynn. Because now those authors have huge platforms.” Unfortunately, “most books don't succeed even with a lot of backing. Combine that with no marketing or publicity, chances are your book isn't going to sell well.”
   If all of this sounds very upsetting to the aspiring author, perhaps there’s hope in my third point. After all, three percent of books still sell somewhere in that 1,000-100,000 range—and that’s enough of an audience to monetize if creator economy technologies are used.
 As the going wisdom states: it only takes 1,000 true fans spending $100/year for a creator to earn a salary of $100,000/year—and there are 83,397 books every year that have at least 1,000 true fans. Theoretically then, an author could release a new chapter every week, charge subscribers $8 or $9 a month, and earn $100,000 a year—from only 1,000 readers.
 Non-fiction writers are already doing it. As evidenced by  this chart by Alexey Guzey, there are plenty of Substack writers who are putting out quality non-fiction content for their followers and monetizing it—earning in the hundreds of thousands of dollars annually, and in some cases millions, just from reader subscriptions!
 But could fiction do the same? That is a yet unanswered question. There  are a few serial  fiction writers on Substack—but none are paid. There are thousands of paid fiction authors on Patreon but only 25 earn more than $1,000/month, only six earn more than $2,000/month, and only one earns more than the $5,000/month ( and she’s already a bestselling author).
 The real success story here is  N. K. Jemisin who was earning $5,068/month publishing fiction on Patreon before she received a traditional publishing contract and went that route instead. But she is the only real case study we have.
 If we don’t yet have enough case studies to determine whether fiction could be successfully serialized and monetized like non-fiction content, we at least know there is a market for it.  Wattpad currently has 90 million users who spend an average of 52 minutes per session reading books online—most Millennial and Gen Z. Similarly,  Archive of our own (AO3) has upwards of 40 million users following fanfiction stories online.
 On both sites, new chapters come out as writers write them and followers read and comment on those chapters in real-time. The problem with both platforms is that readers read for free and authors don’t earn anything from their work—but that is starting to change. With the launch of Wattpad’s Paid Stories two in 2019, readers are starting to pay for chapters using micropayments—it costs three “coins” to unlock the next section of a book.
 Unfortunately, the author does not get to decide whether or not their content is part of the  Paid Stories program—Wattpad does based on how the book performed in its free iteration. And two years into the program Wattpad announced reaching only $1 million in author earnings—split among 550 writers.
  All things being equal, that’s only $1,818 in total earnings, per author, over a two-year period—and all things are not equal. The more likely scenario is that a small percentage of those 550 made up the bulk of the earnings with the rest floundering at the bottom like the rest of us.
 One Wattpad author, who wished to remain anonymous, told me her book reached 20 million free reads on the platform before she was invited to go paid last year. Since then, she has earned a couple million more reads and has averaged $500/month in earnings with her highest month topping out at $1,000. And this is with a YA romance novel—one of the best performing categories on the site.
 If 20 million people are willing to read a book for free and a couple million more are willing to pay for it, but the author still only nets $12,000 or $13,000 in a year—what hope is there for the novelist as a profession? Even when creator technologies are used, there just aren’t enough people willing to pay for fiction for fiction authors to make a living doing it.
 Amazon knows this better than anyone, and perhaps that’s why they announced, in April of 2021, the launch of  Kindle Vella—a Wattpad competitor that allows authors to publish their books serially and earn 50 percent of what readers pay to unlock the next chapter. Because there are so many millions of us who will pour our books onto Amazon in hopes of becoming the next Dan Brown that Amazon will earn a pretty penny of those 50 percent profits—even if we only ever sell 200 copies apiece.
 They’re like the gym membership that makes money on everyone not showing up.
 Alas, I am one of those who can't help but persist in the dream—and I keep that kindling alive with one tiny spark of an idea: that we used to think no one would pay $5/month to subscribe to an individual writer when they could pay $4/month and subscribe to all of  The New York Times—and Substack has since proved that wrong.
 Perhaps that’s why, with my last candle, I still hold hope that we just haven’t proved the case for fiction yet. If you'll indulge my fantasy a while longer....
   If I still harbor some hope that the creator economy might work for fiction authors, the question remains: which is the best platform for fiction novels?
 Certainly, Wattpad and AO3 are obvious contenders for those who want reads over earnings. Both platforms have an enormous fanbase and offer the author a platform on which they can develop a following for their work. As an added benefit, in either case the writer retains the rights to their work.
 (This is not true with Amazon Vella. If an author chooses to publish their novel using the platform it cannot be published anywhere else—and again, Amazon is earning 50 percent of the earnings here which is pretty steep as far as royalties go. Especially without the promise of any additional marketing and with an unknown market.)
 Because of this, Substack and Patreon have the edge when it comes to monetizing serial content (though Wattpad could be a contender later on depending on whether they open up their paid program). And because both platforms allow the author to maintain the rights to their work, there is nothing preventing those authors from putting their books up on Wattpad, AO3, and Kindle after the subscription period.
 Having determined that  I would serialize my own novel as an experiment, releasing one chapter per week from August 2021 through May of 2022, I created both a Substack and a Patreon account to test the waters. Ultimately, though both had their share of merits, neither were ideally suited for fiction.
 Patreon doesn’t have a free pricing tier which means I would have to build my platform elsewhere (Substack, social media, etc.) before attempting to sell into it. Substack  does allow me to have a free newsletter that easily upsells into a paid version, but I don’t have access to all the pricing tiers I can get with Patreon. (Substack only allows me to have one monthly subscription fee, plus a “lump sum” donation bucket.)
 Substack has the right idea—it is far easier to keep an audience on one platform and upsell them than it is to move someone from one platform to another. And yet I know pricing tiers are a must. After all, why would a reader pay $5/month to read four chapters of a book each month when they could buy a whole book on Kindle for $1.99? (This is why there are plenty of writers writing novels on Patreon earning $200/month—and plenty of Kindle authors earning $200 total).
 There has to be added value. That’s why I want my readers to have the option to join an exclusive online community; be mentioned in the acknowledgements section of my book; write the foreword for my book; receive an autographed, collectors edition, hardcover copy of the book when it’s done; attend my wrap party at the end; or elope to a gothic estate in France to write ghost stories together afterward.
 This is, after all, how N.K. Jemisin reached her Patreon success. She had five superfans paying $100/month for signed, printed copies of her books and nine paying $50/month signed-author copies that came out while they were subscribers. That’s $950/month in revenue just from her top 14 fans! And authors can’t afford to miss out on that kind of patronage. (Think of Pottermore—the fandom just keeps on giving.)
 At this point, I have decided to serialize my novel using Substack—the whole process is already built in and has been proven to work for non-fiction authors—but I still don’t know whether I will add pricing tiers via my own custom Stripe account, a Venmo business account, or even by combining my Substack account with Patreon pricing tiers. I think I’m still hoping that Substack will come out with the feature on their own by the time my book publishes on August 1st.
 I have faith that Substack will get there. They have a proof of concept,  a lot of investment coming in, and just this week announced the option to  add sections to publications—which will allow me to have a newsletter separate from the chapters of my novel.
 Even with the help of these emerging technologies, the creator economy might not work for fiction authors—nothing else really has since the invention of the television. But I’m going to run an experiment anyway and see what happens. Because you never know, maybe there’s hope for fiction after all—we just haven’t figured it all out yet.
  As always, thank you so much for reading, and I’ll see you in two weeks!
     I’ve been collecting and following authors who are currently writing fiction on Substack in hopes of learning from them as I get ready to launch mine. Here are a few of the ones I have discovered:
 Monday Morning Mark by  Mark Starlin is one of my favorite newsletters. He writes short, humorous, fiction stories that remind me of   Deep Thoughts by Jack Handey (remember those?). I laugh out loud to myself almost every time.
 The Hound’s Bite shares short gothic stories interspersed with gothic novel chapters by  Zoe Harrington.
  Solar Flow is a subnautical thriller by  K.B. Bailey that I can’t wait to read.
       Saffron and Bear is a novel about a witch and her cat by  Katie Conrad.
 Inykaethel is the memoirs of a vampire that I am very excited to read once it starts.
 And if you’re wondering what order I put that in, the answer is aesthetic. I wanted to make it do that vee thing so that it looks like a flock of geese.
  All of us above (and several more Substack writers) have a discord server where we share all of our Substack writing secrets.  You can join here.
  I’ve decided to no longer tell you what I’m reading because I’ve learned that I read the first part of a book and then decide I don’t want to read it anymore, and I do this lots of times. So I’m going to share only the books I’ve finished.
 Since we’ve last spoken two weeks ago, that’s: no books. Largely because I’m reading about five books right now and it’s getting out of hand. I’ll have more to report next time.
 OH, but I did intensely love an antique book I discovered at a bed & breakfast which was all about flowers. One of my favorite takeaways is that peonies are native to China and mischievous nymphs were said to hide in their petals. It was a very enjoyable book.
  I’m still working on chapter three of the novel I’m currently working on. It wouldn’t normally take me this long to finish a chapter, but I’m wondering how imaginative I want to make it, and that is something I need to decide before I write the rest of it. Writing about the future can go ANYWHERE and I am overwhelmed by all the possibilities! And very excited about them.
 I also wrote this newsletter and did some more advertising for my newsletter in hopes of promoting my first book that will debut in August (SO EXCITED/NERVOUS). I’ll share my results shortly.
title: 去年只有268本书销售超过100k份
title_en: Only 268 books sold more than 100k copies last year

note_en:
A recurring questions that surfaces around the  Future of Coding Communityis  what happened to OpenDoc? why did it fail?
 This post is a summary of reasons found around the web, then I will exploreother implementations similar to OpenDoc to see if there is a general pattern.
 Bias warning: I pick the quotes and the emphasis, read the sources in full toform your own conclusion and let me know!
  The OpenDoc concept was that developers could just write the one piece theywere best at, then let end-users mix and match all of the little pieces offunctionality together as they wished.
  Hence was born OpenDoc, both the technology and the  consortium,consisting primarily of Apple, IBM, and WordPerfect, all companies thatdidn’t like Microsoft very much. All  poured loads of money into theinitiative.
  The Copland team was wary of OpenDoc. I looked at those people as bad guysat the time, but in reality  they were right to be afraid. It’s hard toremember now, but back in 1996 memory (as in RAM) was a big issue.
 The average Mac had about 2 megabytes of memory. OpenDoc wouldn’t run on amachine with less than 4 megs, and realistically, 8 megs was probably whatyou wanted.
  The OpenDoc human interface team had taken it upon themselves to correctthe perceived flaws of the Mac as a modal, application-centric userexperience, and instead adopted a document-centric model for OpenDoc apps.
  It was a noble and interesting idea, but  in retrospect it was a reach, notimportant to the real goals of OpenDoc, and it scared a lot of peopleincluding the developers we were trying to woo.
  It didn’t create a new economy around tiny bits of application code, andthe document-centric model was never allowed to bloom as we had hoped, tothe point where it would differentiate the Mac user experience.
  There are lots of reasons for OpenDoc’s failure, but ultimately it comesdown to the fundamental question of  why Apple was developing thistechnology when no-one in the company really wanted it. The OS group hadmixed feelings, but ultimately didn’t care. Most folks at Claris, Apple’sapplication group,  didn’t want it at all, seeing it as an enabler forcompetition to Claris’s office suite product, ClarisWorks.
 UX quality. If every component is developed by an independent company, who is responsible for unifying it into a nice holistic experience rather than a mishmash?
   Exporting.  Hard to export a "whole document" into a different format if it's made of a bunch of different parts.
 Lack of broad utility.  How common are "compound documents" really? Beyond the classic example of "word doc with images and videos embedded"
 Data format compatibility. If two components both edit spreadsheets but use different data formats, what do you do?
 Historical accident. Turf wars between Microsoft and everyone else,  Steve Jobs ruthlessly prioritizing at Apple, execution failures, etc.
 Despite OpenDoc discouraging vendor lock-in for file formats,  many vendors still insisted on locking down the file formats they used or making them incompatible with other vendors' products
 Lack of buy-in from the developer community:  cumbersome C-based API and obtuse SDKs dissuaded developers from buying into the hype, plus it would have required them to  re-think how their application worked in the first place (what would Photoshop look like as an OpenDoc app?)
 Very little dogfooding: even some of the big partners like IBM and Novell did a  really bad job implementing OpenDoc in their product suites
 OpenDoc made all these assumptions about how things were coded, which werekind of reasonable for an object-oriented program, which ClarisWorks wasnot. We told management that it just wasn't going to work trying to putOpenDoc support into ClarisWorks, especially not without doing a lot ofrewriting first. And they said, well, too bad, do it anyway.
  It was a good idea, it was certainly poorly done, but it still could haveworked in a different codebase.  They had like up to 100 people-engineers,testing, and support and all that-working on OpenDoc. That's too many people.Anytime you have that many people starting something like that, it's usuallypoorly done. They had too many compromises in their design, or just lack offoresight. Integrating it into ClarisWorks was certainly a huge problem.  Itwas not made to be integrated into existing programs like, say, OLE was.
 But they also had ActiveX. And that’s the thing, even a little more so thanOLE, that OpenDoc was aiming it for. And  it was a bad idea from thebeginning. And I kind of sensed something was wrong but it wasn’t until westarted playing it out and realizing how big a project it was and howlittle we had thought about the benefits and what you would actually dowith it. And people tried to address it in various ways.
  But  we were doing it because Microsoft was doing it and we needed to havesomething better.
 If we believe that it was all Steve Job's fault for stopping its developmentearly, because the hardware wasn't there or because of bad execution then  ifsomeone tried something similar again then it should succeed right?
 I went looking for other attempts at the same idea of "[Document] Componentbased software", here are the main contenders and some reasons why they are notthe way we do things right now.
 They are not 100% document based, but since they are more focused/pragmatic atleast they should have succeeded being used as a better way to reuse componentsacross applications.
 Even after simplification, users still required controls to implement aboutsix core interfaces. In response to this complexity, Microsoft producedwizards, ATL base classes, macros and C++ language extensions to make itsimpler to write controls.
  Such controls,  in practice, ran only on Windows, and separate controls were required for each supported platform
  The ActiveX security model relied almost entirely on identifying trusted component developers using a code signing technology
  Identified code would then  run inside the web browser with full permissions,meaning that any bug in the code was a potential security issue; thiscontrasts with the sandboxing already used in Java at the time.
   The need for ActiveX controls has been significantly reduced by HTML5-eracapabilities, which also produces interoperable code across browsers.
 If the problem was the business model but the technology was really powerfulthen it should not be a problem for up-and-coming open source desktopenvironments with a user base consisting mostly on power users and developersthat would benefit from code reuse.
  An interesting fact is that Gnome used to be an acronym: GNUNetwork Object Model Environment, the Object Model idea was in the name itself.
 I didn't notice until now that the name KParts seems to come from OpenDoc's Parts concept.
  A KPart is a dynamically loadable module which  provides an embeddable document orcontrol view including associated menu and toolbar actions. A broker returnsKPart objects for certain data or service types to the requesting application. KParts are for example used for embedding an image viewer into the web browseror for embedding a spread sheet object into the word processor.
    Bonobo is a set of interfaces for providing application embedding andin-place activation which are being defined.  The Bonobo interfaces andinteractions are modeled after the OLE2 and OpenDoc interfaces.
  Reusable controls: Another set of Bonobo interfaces deal with reusablecontrols. This is similar to Sun's JavaBeans and Microsoft Active-X.
    Nautilus used Bonobo to componentise functionality outside the filemanagement view, like the web rendering, or the audio playback and musicalbum view.
  This was the heyday of the component era of GNOME, and  while its promisesof shiny new functionality were attractive to both platform and applicationdevelopers, the end result was by and large one of untapped potential.Componentisation requires a large initial effort in designing thearchitecture of an application, and it’s really hard to introduce after thefact without laying waste to working code.
  As an initial roadblock  it poorly fits with the “scratch your own itch”approach of free and open source software. Additionally it requires notjust a higher level of discipline in the component design and engineering,it also depends on comprehensive and extensive documentation, somethingthat has always been the Achille’s Heel of many an open source project.
  Sun’s usability engineer Calum Benson presented the results of the firstround of user testing on GNOME 1.4, and while the results were encouragingin some areas,  they laid bare the limits of the current design approach ofa mish-mash of components. If GNOME wanted to be usable by professionals,not curating the offering of the desktop was not a sustainable option anymore.
  The consistency, or lack thereof, of the environment was one of the issuesthat the testing immediately identified: identical functionality waslabelled differently depending on the component; settings like the fontsto be used by the desktop were split across various places; duplication offunctionality, mostly for the sake of duplication, was rampant. Case inpoint: the GNOME panel shipped with not one, not two, but five differentclock applets—including, of course, the binary clock
  Additionally,  all those clocks, like all the panel applets, could be addedand removed by sheer accident, clicking around on the desktop. The panelitself could simply go away, and there was no way to revert to a workingstate without nuking the settings for the user.
 KParts seems to still be around, you can see a  list of actively developed KParts on KDE's GitHub account, Bonobo was replaced by  DBuswhich is a Message-oriented abstraction not related to OpenDoc concepts as far as I know.
 Web Components is a suite of different technologies allowing you to create reusable custom elements — with their functionality encapsulated away from the rest of your code — and utilize them in your web apps.
 Web Components have been around for a while and developers seem to be split in two campsregarding how useful it is.
 We will see with time if it becomes the main way to do web applications and anew attempt at OpenDoc, this time on the web.
 Still not sure which were the main reasons, but many of them aren't shared by later attempts:
  The conclusion is that there's no conclusion, the case is still open, what do you think?
title: 为什么Opendoc失败，然后再次失败了3次？
title_en: Why OpenDoc failed, and then failed 3 more times?

似乎在美国，至少，依靠有针对性的移动广告的App开发人员和广告商依赖于收入的最严重的恐惧：本周发布的分析数据表明，美国用户选择选择退出追踪96％的时间在iOS 14.5之后。
  当Apple发布IOS 14.5上个月后，它开始强制执行称为App Tracking透明度的策略。现在需要iPhone，iPad和Apple TV应用程序来申请用户＆＃39;使用像IDFA（广告商ID）这样的技术的许可来跟踪这些用户＆＃39;用于多个应用程序的活动，用于数据收集和广告目标目的。这种变化符合Facebook这样的公司的激烈抵抗，其市场优势和收入流是建立在利用用户＆＃39;数据为这些用户定位最有效的广告。 Facebook就拿出了全页报纸广告，声称改变不仅仅伤害了Facebook，而且会摧毁世界各地的小企业。不久之后，Apple Ceo Ceo Tim Cook参加了数据隐私会议，并发表了一个严厉批评Facebook＆＃39;商业模式的演讲。
    尽管如此，Facebook和其他人遵守了苹果＆＃39;避免从iPhone＆＃39; S App Store拒绝，尽管一些应用程序呈现了一个屏幕，解释了用户在Apple授权提示之前选择为什么选择选择以选择以选择或出现出来。
 这种新数据来自Verizon拥有的丝网分析，声称将用于超过一百万的移动应用程序。 Flurry表示，它将每天更新数据，因此追随者可以看到它的进展情况。
 基于来自这一百万个应用程序的数据，Flurry Analytics表示，美国用户同意仅被追踪4％的时间。全球数量在12％的百分比明显高，但仍然低于一些广告公司＆＃39;估计。
 来自Flurry Analytics的数据显示用户拒绝跟踪的速率比在IOS 14.5之前进行的调查预测的速率更高。其中一个调查发现，只害羞为40％，而不是4％，将在促使时追踪。
 Flurry Analytics＆＃39;尽管如此，数据不打破应用程序，所以它不可能从这个数据中知道这些数据，无论数字都是针对应用程序跟踪选择的数字倾斜，例如，用户＆＃39;对Facebook的不信任。它和＃39;可能的用户更信任某些类型的应用程序，但数据不可用。
note_en:
It seems that in the United States, at least, app developers and advertisers who rely on targeted mobile advertising for revenue are seeing their worst fears realized:  Analytics data published this week suggests that US users choose to opt out of tracking 96 percent of the time in the wake of iOS 14.5.
  When Apple  released iOS 14.5 late last month, it began enforcing a policy called App Tracking Transparency. iPhone, iPad, and Apple TV apps are now required to request users' permission to use techniques like IDFA (ID for Advertisers) to track those users' activity across multiple apps for data collection and ad targeting purposes. The change met fierce resistance from companies like Facebook, whose market advantages and revenue streams are built on leveraging users' data to target the most effective ads at those users. Facebook went so far as to take out full-page newspaper ads claiming that the change would not just hurt Facebook but would destroy small businesses around the world. Shortly after, Apple CEO Tim Cook attended a data privacy conference  and delivered a speech that harshly criticized Facebook's business model.
    Nonetheless, Facebook and others have complied with Apple's new rule to avoid being rejected from the iPhone's App Store, though some apps present a screen explaining why users should opt in before the Apple-mandated prompt to opt in or out appears.
 This new data comes from Verizon-owned Flurry Analytics, which claims to be used in more than one million mobile apps. Flurry says it will update the data daily so followers can see the trend as it progresses.
 Based on the data from those one million apps, Flurry Analytics says US users agree to be tracked only four percent of the time. The global number is significantly higher at 12 percent, but that's still below some advertising companies' estimates.
 The data from Flurry Analytics shows users rejecting tracking at much higher rates than were predicted by surveys that were conducted before iOS 14.5 went live. One of those surveys found that  just shy of 40 percent, not 4 percent, would opt in to tracking when prompted.
 Flurry Analytics' data doesn't break things down by app, though, so it's impossible to know from this data whether the numbers are skewed against app tracking opt-in by, say, users' distrust of Facebook. It's possible users are being more trusting of some types of apps than others, but that data is not available.
title: 96％的美国用户选择退出IOS 14.5中的应用程序跟踪，分析查找
title_en: 96% of US users opt out of app tracking in iOS 14.5, analytics find

某些行业培育一种专业的专业人士，致力于尊敬是荣誉的徽章。这种现象正在上升，但它是可持续的吗？
  40小时的工作周已经死了。但人们不仅仅是工作时间较长。他们每周阻止极端的工作，每周记录60多小时，并持久的24/7客户需求，更紧密的截止日期，并且不可预测的工作流动。
  然而，许多极端的工人们不觉得剥削 - 他们觉得崇高。有些人喜欢智力挑战和实现大事的令人兴奋。其他人助长了境内与地区的超大补偿包和地位。
  极端工作燃料工人的肾上腺素高，整个国家的竞争性竞争性竞争性竞争力，在工业中突破，如金融服务，咨询，医学，法律和制造业。
  尽管如此，惠顾仍然会看到一丝危险：极端工人的健康问题和家庭困境，当极端工作持有人遭受不可避免的倦怠或缺陷时，极端工作人员的侵蚀性企业生产力。
  极端工作可以在短期内丰富贵公司。但它可以随着长期的成本而生活吗？
      除了60多个小时的几周外，24/7客户需求，不可预测的工作流动，以及紧张的截止日期，许多极限工作都有这些特点： 
竞争压力。由于公司官员的职位较少（归功于Merger Mania）和人才涌入（由于多样性努力），高级工作的竞争加剧。人们强烈激励，以赢得李子角色的竞争对手。
  “极端”的精神。许多人拥抱“极端”的普遍的社会 - 以令人生气的运动和基于特技的现实电视节目的形象而成。精神博士陷入了商业制作的极限工作看起来迷人和理想。
  通信技术。技术促使人们期望和行为的转变。他们不断地将边界放在工作日周围，而是经常粘在手机和黑莓手机上。
  作为社会中心工作。当人们最好的朋友和大多数刺激的遭遇都在办公室时，他们很乐意迟到。他们的家人和个人生活萎缩。工作，而不是家，成为人们钦佩的地方，尊重。
  更多基于知识的工作。公司现在充满了使用他们大脑的人而不是他们的肌肉。他们喜欢与同龄人交换想法，并发现长时间刺激，而不是痛苦。
  全球化。随着公司获得全球跨境的全球跨国公司，需要监督多个时区的工作，扩大管理者的旅行要求和工作日长度。
      35％的25-34岁的极端工人表示，他们可能会在两年内留下工作。 
超过69％的作者的学习受访者认为，如果他们少得多，他们会更健康。
  58％的人认为他们的极端工作妨碍了与孩子的强烈关系。
  65％的受访者表示，如果它要求更多的能量，他们会拒绝促销。
    金融分析师我们将五年前从印度孟买呼吁苏希尔致电。他在纽约的一家主要商业银行工作。夏季，当他每周换90个小时时，他是他的“轻盈”季节。剩下的一年中，他每周上班120小时 - 睡觉，吃，娱乐和（他微笑）沐浴只留下48个小时。即使他没有特别紧迫的话，苏迪尔也在办公室迟到了。他的生命存在是专业的危害 - 但值得的是：作为一个23岁的第一份工作，他是美国中产民的前6％。
  越高，图腾杆，乔（不是他的真实姓名）已经通过公司排名来到主要银行的董事总经理。乔认为他的工作量随着他搬到了升起而变得更轻，但相反的情况发生了：他现在从多个地方每周工作了六或七天。他在纽约保留一套公寓，在两天工作，在路上另外三四天。只有周末他看到他的妻子和三个孩子 - 居住在康涅狄格州。即便如此，他在周六和星期天的半夜被拨打电话，并在一瞬间的通知中苍蝇看到客户。 “我们结婚的第一年，”乔的妻子回忆说：“我们不得不重新排列我的祖母的葬礼，以便他不会错过会议。”
  Ming Mei是董事总经理和Prologis执行委员会成员，这是一个快速增长的房地产投资信托，在亚洲广泛的行动。 Mei负责在中国的扩张，在过去三年中，他将Prologis的投资组合基础从零到1000万平方英尺的特性。他的工作的要求是巨大的。他与中国政府官员谈判，他经常将五个城市包装为六天的商务旅行。这些旅行可以是艰苦的背靠背会议溢出到后期晚餐，其中关键关系培养和巩固。尽管有压力和速度，但梅介绍了他的工作，发光术语：“在没有人这样做的市场中建立这一业务之前是非常令人兴奋的。和重要。我们建立了对中国增长至关重要的分销中心 - 他们有助于经济的整体前景。“
  Jonelle Salter同样热情地对她的工作。 Jonelle在BP的海上安装经理（OIM）知道它希望负责80名工人在北海的石油平台上的健康和安全。在这份工作中会面临任何人的压力之上，她有一些独特的管理挑战。作为第一个成为BP的Oim的黑人女性，Jonelle有时必须去额外的长度来建立她在这个男性主导的环境中的权威。但她喜欢成为一个先锋和学分，以便在肢体上出去，找到一位女性导师（Gro Kielland，Bc挪威的董事总经理），以帮助她通过粗糙的补丁。 Jonelle雄辩地谈论了她工作挑战的刺激。 “你训练和火车，但你仍然不知道你是否会发生紧急情况 - 无论你是否能召唤正确的领导力，”她说。 “这是一种测试。当你通过时，你觉得很好。“ 
Sudhir，Joe，Mei和Jonelle正在取得成功，我们来到了“极端工作”，他们并不孤单。在经济上，有高收入的专业人士，其工作已经变得全力以赴。他们投入他们的职业的令人徒步时间只能通过他们收到的超级奖励匹配。
  这些专业人士是否构成了新品种？不是完全。非常苛刻和重要的工作始终与工作方式一起工作，他们在不需要存在的地方创造它们。然而，有一个差异。不再是社会可怜的无人机和掌上者，今天的超级知识专业人士是作为宇宙的道路勇士和大师的重新评估。他们的劳动越来越长，承担更多责任，比以往任何时候都更加奢侈 - 他们的数字正在增长。
  我们对极端工作的研究是我们在2004年2月推出的隐藏脑排水工作队的项目，现在已经开始了。 2005年末，四家工作队的成员公司 - 美国运通，BP，Prologis和UBS-赞助了两次大型调查，目前这些日子旨在“映射”的形状和范围。我们还进行了深入的定性研究 - 焦点小组和访谈 - 获得躺在极端工作模型的态度和动机（侧边栏“研究极端工作”提供更多细节）。然后，我们考虑了与大规模结构转变有关的数据，使得高赌注就业是美国经济和文化的更加突出的特征。从这个询问中出现的是一种复杂的职业奖励在许多方面是一个复杂的画面，但并非没有个人和社会的危险。
      最明确的第一件事是成功的专业人士比以往任何时候都更加努力。这是40小时的工作周，似乎是过去的一件事。即使是60小时的工作周，曾经前面的路径，现在几乎被认为是兼职，因为最近的财富杂志文章把它放了。我们的数据显示，62％的高收入个人每周工作50多小时，每周工作35％以上，每周有10％以上工作80多小时。在一个典型的一小时通勤中添加，一个60小时的工作周转换为凌晨7点离开房子，每周五天晚上9点回家。如果我们专注于拥有我们认为极端工作的工作人员的子集（基于责任和其他属性的指定以外的薪酬），那么时间更加惩罚。其中大多数（56％）每周工作70小时或更长时间，而9％的工作100小时或更长时间。
  这是多么戏剧性是从过去的跳跃？没有关于实际行为的纵向数据，我们必须依靠我们的受访者来衡量增加。在极端的求职者中，48％的人表示，他们平均每周工作16.6个小时，而不是五年前的时间。该发现与扩大工作周的其他研究一致，包括最近，彼得Kuhn和国家经济研究局的费尔南多罗桑的研究。 （在大学教育人士在美国全职工作，Kuhn和Lozano报告中，那些50小时的人在1980年和2001年间增加到30.2％至30.5％。）
  与此同时，假期似乎萎缩。在极端就业人群中，每年42％或更少的假期休假时间比他们正式题为 -  55％的索赔，他们不得不定期取消假期计划。“而且，他们说没有人强迫他们这样做。没有休息的漫长时间和数月是自由裁量权的。 Jay（不是他的真名）是一家主要娱乐公司的创意高管，是一个案例。他不记得他最后一次度假，他有的话，但委派了他的社会生活。他是通过将他公司带入新的创意空间游戏的愿景驱动 - 他的心灵和灵魂在这个使命中被包裹起来，这已成为他身份的定义元素。 “如果我能够为工作室工作，”他告诉我们没有1点的热情，“我将成为第一个搞清楚的人。曾经尝试过的其他人失败了。这是我的珠穆朗玛峰。“
  让我们备份一会儿，澄清我们在磨损的长时间工作和极端工作之间做出的区别。我们的定义从扩展的焦点小组讨论中增加，不仅考虑到几小时（当然，薪水），而且还考虑了使这些职位特别压力的压力。我们确定了极端职位的十大特征，并决定将被告作为一个极端的求职者分类为一个极端的求职者，如果他或她在每周工作60小时或更长时间（见侧边栏“的最重要内部“）。通过本标准，美国的高收入人员的21％是我们调查的备受工作。 （在我们对全球公司工作的专业人士调查中，这个数字上升至45％。） 
事实是，极端工作不再是罕见的。我们的数据揭示了在年龄，性别，行业和大陆的高级小管家专业人员工作压力巨大增加。我们发现的极限工作，分布在经济中的大型制造公司以及华尔街，在医学和法律中的娱乐和媒体，咨询和会计。
  鉴于这种越来越极端的工作模式，人们可能会想象我们的研究也发现了一个很多烧毁和痛苦的专业人士。事实上，相反相反。我们美国样本中的绝大多数极端工作人员（66％）表示他们喜欢他们的工作 - 并在全球公司调查中，这个数字上升到76％。远远没有看到自己是工作狂需要救援的，极端工人就像荣誉徽章一样穿着他们的承诺。为了利用文化评论家凯瑟琳奥伦斯坦的话，这些就业机会构成了“美国类固醇的美国梦”。有很少的受害感。近三分之二的极端工人承认压力和速度是自我造成的 - 一种人格的函数。通过和大，极端专业人士感到乐意;他们感到崇高。
    每个极端工人都有自己的努力的理由。许多人喜欢智力挑战和实现大事的令人兴奋。其他人被超大赔偿包，辉煌的同事和承认和尊重与该领土上的。当我们向调查受访者要求我们的调查受访者，最重要的是，最多的因素。 （见展览“为什么你这样做？”）
    请注意对答复的关系有多。对于男性来说，在刺激/挑战和高质量的同事之后，薪酬在激励者列表中排名第三。对于女性来说，补偿是五分之一或最后一个。 Debra Langford的以下评论是我们在访谈中听到的典型：“并不是那个赔偿不是最重要的 - 它显然很重要。作为非洲裔美国单身女性，金融责任，我必须在职业选择中成为战略性。当我随着时代华纳接受这个职位时，我有责任识别公司的高级职位，我知道好处将超越纯粹的财务状况。这是一个重要的地位，因为我的工作价值以及我为我的努力收到的认可和支持是令人难以置信的奖励。“
  同样，美国运通公开部长的苏珊Sobbott选择了Amex，因为她想成为她相信的特派团的一部分，以及专门的团队的一部分。 “我曾经是华尔街的金融分析师，”她说。 “在我的MBA之后，我称之为投资银行并考虑咨询。我想到了通常在这些领域工作的大笔薪水。但对我来说，在美元符号之后选择道路的最重要的事情 - 比Zeros更重要 - 找到一个我可以与有才华的人一起工作以创造进一步成为一个伟大品牌的领先商业策略。“
  有趣的是，这些动机是，应该指出的是，关于工作的个人决定不是在真空中进行的。在宏观层面，极端工作崛起背后的原因是结构性的;这是全球经济环境中彻底变化的结果。这些变化 - 包括提高竞争压力，大大改善的通信技术，以及强大的方式交叉。
    首先，竞争变得更加激烈，无论是在个人专业人士和公司水平的水平。在公司内，合并狂热和大幅扁平的等级的合并效果一直是为了对任何特定的促销活动进行更大的工人互动筹码。催化剂报告称，2005年在财富500岁以下的企业官员职位比早年前有368次（从11,241到10,873人下降） - 这意味着对高级职位的竞争变得更加强硬。加入这一点，通过妇女的大规模运动进入劳动力和公司持续的多样性努力，这是令人兴奋的人才。这两个因素都提高了竞争水平。由于公司增长较深，我们看到Louis Uchitelle在一次性美国描述的拒绝工作保障中。与此同时，人们较少的肩膀上的责任较少。副主席Paul是一家大型四大会计师事务所的副主席，告诉我们他的工作如此巨大，每天都有更多的事情。 “Clincher，”他说，“这是我没有得到的东西的重要性比曾经是的。” 
经济学家Robert Frank和Philip Cook认为，越来越多，我们的经济经营者通过“赢家采取所有”规则。这是更容易层次结构中存在的动态。由于略微的性能边缘产生奖励（这些天授予CEO的Gargantuan薪水），因此有着强大的动力，以逐步地工作多于一个人的竞争对手。小时和努力相应地棘手。考虑一下这是多么不同的电视节目，如将其留给海狸和Ozzie和Harriet，当一个在9和5小时之间合理富有成效的专业人士时，如果通过级别的不确定上升，可以稳定地依靠稳定。 20世纪50年代的专业模式在许多方面，更善良，但对于任何愿意努力工作的年轻土耳其人才能获得奖励，这是一个强烈挫折的源泉。今天的模型由年轻的土耳其人的规则运作：美国梦中并不是为了成为Ozzie Nelson。这是关于唐纳德特朗普。
  越来越多地，年轻的土耳其人实际上是年轻的印第安人或年轻的中文或任何聪明的和勤劳的人口提供劳动力成本优势。失去就业机会的威胁到外包安排是另一个司机在极端工作的兴起。最后，医疗保险保险和其他福利的攀登成本 - “非豁免”专业人员在美国 - 让公司渴望在员工之前尽可能多地挤压员工们在员工中尽可能多地挤压员工完全加载的薪水。
    虽然公司的竞争压力正在制定极端的工作，但更广泛的社会的其他变化使他们更具吸引力。奥伦斯坦指出了迹象表明，普遍拥抱的民众文化“极端”精神。特别是体育尤其变得非常受欢迎 - 他们拥有自己的奥运会版本，简单地作为X游戏，于1995年由ESPN创建。现实电视节目恐惧因素为沙发土豆替代刺激，让普通人在极端特技中的测试中。社区健康俱乐部现在为那些厌恶沉闷的运动常规提供攀岩墙和跆拳道课程。
  我们首次听到四年前在伦敦的投资银行的高级银行家采访时，在接受玛丽莲的采访时聆讯到白领的“极端”一词。玛丽莲被极限运动所吸引：跳伞，滑雪板，铁人三项，蹦极跳跃，冲浪，登山 - 任何提供肾上腺素的东西和危险的元素。她急切地推荐Jon Krakauer进入瘦身（Amateur山地登山者的一个不合适的旅行）作为窗户，为什么人们将自己推向体质耐力的极限。玛丽莲在极端运动和她的生命之间看到了像投资银行家之间的相似之处。首先，有非凡的时间需求和性能压力。七十小时的工作周，艰苦的旅行要求，无情的底线压力不断地将她的极限推向身体和智力。其次，有诱惑这项工作。很像极端运动，投资银行令人兴奋，诱人。玛丽莲告诉我们，“它给了我这个匆忙。像药物一样，它是上瘾的。“
  在极端运动的世界里，越来越大胆，苛刻，而且 - 这是说壮举的壮举，我们对运动员的敬畏越大。我们欣赏极端运动员的人才，技能和勇气，也是将他或她与人群分开的哈布里斯。高赌注和危险定义了极端体育挑战，最终的物理较少而不是关于山峰或大波浪的存在而不是关于内部强度和测试一个人的限制。在一个致命这种运动员的流行文化中，极端的精神使其进入另一端的方式并不奇怪
